name: lemon balm
mechanism: Mechanism is not yet established with high confidence; reported activity is likely multi-component and may vary by extract chemistry and dose.
interactions: May potentiate: May increase sedation when combined with CNS depressants
safetyNotes: Safety profile depends on species identity, preparation quality, dose, and co-medications; start low, avoid prolonged high-dose use without supervision, and stop if adverse effects occur.

sources.title: PubMed search: lemon balm
sources.note: Literature index for pharmacology and safety evidence

description: Lemon balm is often selected for gentle calming support when users want a lighter daytime or evening option than strongly sedating herbs.
dosage: Use consistent tea or extract servings for several days before increasing amount; potency varies by form.
duration: Commonly used daily in short-to-medium routines, especially during stress-heavy periods.
preparation: Tea and tincture are common entry formats; capsules are used for travel-friendly consistency.

therapeuticUses: Gentle calm support without strong sedation
Daytime stress-load smoothing with low-friction tea use
Bridge option between chamomile and valerian-style intensity
categoryUseContext: Used in calm-focus crossover routines, tea-first stress support, and lighter evening wind-down protocols.
evidenceLevel: Early-to-moderate evidence for calming contexts, with strongest practical value in tolerability and routine adherence.
